title: Stop a Nosebleed
summary: Stop a nosebleed safely using the correct head position and pressure technique.
learnerPromise: Stop a nosebleed quickly and safely.
whyItMatters: Nosebleeds can happen from dry air, allergies, or minor bumps. Knowing the right way to handle one helps you stay calm and stop the bleeding faster.
realLifeUses: Getting a nosebleed in dry winter weather
Having a minor nosebleed after sneezing repeatedly
Helping a friend or sibling who gets a nosebleed
Dealing with a nosebleed during sports or exercise
youWillLearn: The correct sitting position for a nosebleed
Where to pinch the nose to slow bleeding
What to do after the bleeding stops
miniChallenge: Practice the correct head position and pressure technique so you know it if it happens.
steps: Sit up straight and lean slightly forward. Do not tilt your head back.
Pinch the soft part of your nose, just below the bony bridge, using your thumb and finger.
Hold steady pressure for at least 10 minutes without checking too often.
Breathe through your mouth while you wait.
After the bleeding stops, avoid blowing your nose or picking at it for several hours.
completionCriteria: You know to lean forward, not backward
You can find the correct spot to pinch on the nose
You understand to hold pressure for at least 10 minutes
commonProblems: The bleeding does not stop after 15 to 20 minutes — seek medical help
You feel dizzy or lightheaded — sit down and ask someone nearby for help
Nosebleeds happen often — talk to a doctor about possible causes
tips: Tilting your head back can cause blood to run down the throat — lean forward instead
Using a cool compress on the nose bridge may help while you pinch
A small amount of saline gel inside the nostrils can help prevent dry-air nosebleeds
helpBoundary: Seek medical help if the nosebleed lasts longer than 20 minutes or follows a head injury.
